**S**ales is ALWAYS first
• Can't produce without knowing demand

**P**roduction follows sales
• Adjust for desired inventory changes

**D**irect costs follow production
• Materials, labor, overhead

**C**ash comes from all the above
• Timing of receipts and payments

sections.title: Production Budget
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Determine units to produce

**Formula:**

Units to Produce = 
  Budgeted Sales Units
+ Desired Ending Inventory
- Beginning Inventory

**Example:**
Sales forecast: 10,000 units
Desired ending FG: 2,000 units
Beginning FG: 1,500 units

Production = 10,000 + 2,000 - 1,500 = 10,500 units

**Key:** Production adjusts for inventory policy

sections.title: Cash Budget
sections.content:
**The Most Critical Budget:**
Shows whether cash will be sufficient

**Format:**

**Beginning Cash Balance**
+ Cash Receipts
  • Collections from sales
  • Other income
= Cash Available

- Cash Disbursements
  • Raw materials
  • Labor
  • Overhead
  • Selling & admin
  • Capital expenditures
  • Taxes, dividends, debt payments
= Ending Cash Balance (before financing)

+/- Financing
  • Borrowing or repayment
= Ending Cash Balance

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Collection Patterns
sections.content:
**Cash receipts ≠ Sales!**

**Typical Collection Pattern:**
• 30% collected in month of sale
• 60% collected in following month
• 10% collected two months after

**Example: January Sales = $100,000**
January collections: $30,000
February collections: $60,000
March collections: $10,000

**Exam Tip:** Read the collection pattern carefully!
May also include cash discounts or bad debts

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Master budget = operating budgets + financial budgets
Sales budget is the foundation - all others depend on it
Production = Sales + Ending inventory - Beginning inventory
Direct costs (materials, labor, overhead) follow production budget
Cash budget is critical for liquidity planning
Cash receipts depend on collection patterns, not sales timing
Pro forma statements summarize budget results

knowledgeCheck.question: A company forecasts sales of 15,000 units for the quarter. Beginning finished goods inventory is 3,000 units and the company wants ending inventory of 4,000 units. How many units should be produced?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Production = Sales + Ending inventory - Beginning inventory = 15,000 + 4,000 - 3,000 = 16,000 units. The company must produce enough to sell AND increase inventory by 1,000 units.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Production must cover both sales and the change in inventory levels.

title: Rolling Forecasts and Beyond Budgeting
description: Modern approaches that replace or supplement traditional annual budgets

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional annual budgets become stale quickly! Rolling forecasts and beyond budgeting approaches give organizations agility to respond to changing conditions. CMAs must understand these modern alternatives for effective performance management.

sections.title: Problems with Traditional Budgeting
items: **Outdated quickly:** Annual budgets often obsolete within months
**Time-consuming:** Takes 4-6 months to prepare
**Gaming behavior:** Managers pad budgets, lowball targets
**Annual focus:** Ignores beyond year-end
**Fixed mindset:** Treated as contracts, not guidelines
**Encourages spending:** Use it or lose it mentality

sections.title: Rolling Forecasts
sections.content:
**Definition:** Continuous planning that always looks a set period ahead

**How It Works:**
• Typically 12-18 month horizon
• Add a new period as one expires
• Updated monthly or quarterly
• Always looking forward same distance

**Example: 12-Month Rolling Forecast**
• January: Forecast Jan-Dec
• February: Drop Jan, add next Jan (Feb Y1 - Jan Y2)
• March: Drop Feb, add next Feb (Mar Y1 - Feb Y2)
• ...always 12 months ahead

**Benefits:**
• Never using stale assumptions
• Continuous strategic conversation
• Better visibility into future

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Rolling vs Static
sections.content:
**"ROLL" - Rolling Forecast Advantages:**

**R**eal-time updates (not annual)
**O**ngoing horizon (always same distance ahead)
**L**ess detailed (focus on key drivers)
**L**imits gaming (too frequent to manipulate)

**Think of it as a car's headlights - always illuminating the same distance ahead as you move forward!**

sections.title: Beyond Budgeting Principles
sections.content:
**A management model that eliminates traditional budgets:**

**12 Principles in Two Categories:**

**Leadership Principles:**
1. Customers - Focus on customer outcomes
2. Organization - Self-governing teams
3. Responsibility - Front-line autonomy
4. Autonomy - Freedom to act
5. Values - Shared values guide behavior
6. Transparency - Open information

**Process Principles:**
7. Goals - Relative, not fixed targets
8. Rewards - Team-based, hindsight
9. Planning - Continuous, inclusive
10. Coordination - Dynamic, not annual
11. Resources - Available when needed
12. Controls - Relative KPIs

sections.title: Relative Performance Measures
sections.content:
**Beyond budgeting uses relative targets:**

**Traditional:** Beat budget of 5% growth
**Beyond Budgeting:** Beat competitors

**Examples:**
• Top quartile in industry for ROI
• Better customer satisfaction than last year
• Higher market share than competitors
• Cost improvement vs. prior period

**Advantages:**
• No sandbagging
• Adjusts automatically to conditions
• External focus
• Continuous improvement mindset

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Beyond Budgeting Misconception
sections.content:
**Beyond budgeting does NOT mean:**
• No planning (there is planning)
• No targets (there are targets - just relative ones)
• No accountability (stronger, based on outcomes)
• No forecasting (rolling forecasts central)

**It DOES mean:**
• No fixed annual budget contract
• Decentralized decision-making
• Adaptive resource allocation
• Relative performance evaluation

**Key companies:** Handelsbanken, Google (partial), Unilever (partial)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Traditional budgets become outdated and encourage gaming
Rolling forecasts maintain constant planning horizon
Add a new period each time one expires
Beyond budgeting replaces fixed budgets with adaptive management
Uses relative targets (vs competitors, vs prior periods)
Decentralizes authority and resource allocation
Still requires planning, forecasting, and accountability

knowledgeCheck.question: A company uses an 18-month rolling forecast updated quarterly. At the end of Q1, what would they do?
options: Create an entirely new 18-month forecast from scratch
Update the existing forecast and add a new quarter at the end
Compare actual results to the original forecast
Lock in the next 6 months and leave the rest unchanged
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With a rolling forecast, as one quarter is completed, a new quarter is added at the end to maintain the 18-month horizon. The forecast is also updated for any changes in assumptions. This is different from a static budget which would just compare to original.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Rolling means always maintaining the same horizon length by adding as time passes.

title: Integrating Capital Budget with Operating Budget
description: Connect long-term capital decisions with annual operating plans and cash budgets

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capital investments drive operating performance! New equipment affects depreciation, maintenance costs, labor needs, and capacity. CMAs ensure the operational and financial impacts of capital decisions flow through all related budgets.

sections.title: Budget Hierarchy
sections.content:
**Strategic Plan (3-5+ years)**
• Long-term direction
• Major capital initiatives
↓
**Capital Budget (1-5 years)**
• Specific investments approved
• Timing and amounts
↓
**Operating Budget (1 year)**
• Annual revenue and expenses
• Reflects capital impacts
↓
**Cash Budget (monthly/quarterly)**
• Cash timing effects
• Financing needs

sections.title: Capital Budget Impacts on Operating Budget
🧠 Memory Aid: Capital to Cash
sections.content:
**"DORIC" - How Capital Flows to Cash Budget:**

**D**epreciation - Non-cash, so add back
**O**perating costs - New maintenance, utilities
**R**evenue changes - Capacity impacts
**I**nterest expense - Debt-financed purchases
**C**apital expenditure - Cash outflow when purchased

**Capital budget triggers multiple downstream changes!**

sections.title: Cash Budget Integration
sections.content:
**Capital Expenditures in Cash Budget:**

**Cash Outflows:**
• Equipment purchases (when paid, not when depreciated)
• Installation costs
• Working capital increases
• Financing payments if debt-funded

**Cash Inflows (if asset sold):**
• Proceeds from asset sales
• Tax effects from gains/losses

**Timing Considerations:**
• Often large lump-sum payments
• May require temporary financing
• Plan for installation timing
• Consider deposits/progress payments

sections.title: Example: New Equipment
sections.content:
**Capital Decision:**
Purchase $600,000 machine
Useful life: 10 years (straight-line)
Expected labor savings: $100,000/year
Additional maintenance: $15,000/year

**Operating Budget Changes:**
• Depreciation: +$60,000/year
• Labor cost: -$100,000/year
• Maintenance: +$15,000/year
• Net operating income impact: +$25,000

**Cash Budget Changes:**
• Year 1 cash outflow: $600,000
• Annual cash savings: $85,000 ($100K - $15K)
• (Depreciation is non-cash!)

**Note the difference between profit and cash impact!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Depreciation
sections.content:
**Depreciation affects budgets differently:**

**Operating Budget (Income Statement):**
• Depreciation IS an expense
• Reduces operating income

**Cash Budget:**
• Depreciation is NOT a cash outflow
• Cash outflow happened when asset was purchased
• Must ADD BACK to net income for cash flow

**Common Error:**
Students double-count depreciation by:
1. Including purchase in cash budget
2. Also deducting depreciation in cash budget

**Rule:** Purchase = cash outflow; Depreciation = non-cash

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital decisions flow through to operating and cash budgets
New equipment affects depreciation, maintenance, labor
Cash budget shows actual payment timing, not depreciation
Depreciation is expense for operating budget but not cash outflow
Capital expenditures often lump-sum cash impacts
Operating savings from capital may differ from cash savings
Working capital needs may accompany capital investments

knowledgeCheck.question: A company purchases equipment for $120,000 with a 4-year life. Annual labor savings will be $40,000. How much should appear in the Year 1 CASH budget as the net effect?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Year 1 cash: Equipment purchase $(120,000) + Labor savings $40,000 = $(80,000) net cash outflow. Depreciation ($30,000/year) is not a cash flow. Cash budget reflects actual cash movements, not accounting expenses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Ignore depreciation in cash calculations - it's non-cash. Focus on the purchase and operating cost savings.

title: Performance-Based and Activity-Based Budgeting
description: Compare advanced budgeting methods beyond traditional approaches

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional budgeting has critics! Modern approaches like ABB, ZBB, and Beyond Budgeting address limitations of incremental budgeting. CMAs must know when each approach fits and how to implement them effectively.

sections.title: Traditional Budgeting Limitations
sections.content:
**Problems with Incremental Budgeting:**

• **Historical anchor:** Last year + X%
• **Bloated costs:** Inefficiencies carry forward
• **Gaming:** Spend it or lose it
• **Slow:** Annual cycle too long
• **Promotes silos:** Department focus vs. enterprise

**Result:**
Budgets that don't drive performance improvement
Managers focus on "hitting budget" vs. value creation

**Alternative approaches attempt to fix these issues

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Zero-Based Budgeting
sections.content:
**ZBB Process - Start from ZERO!**

**Step 1:** Define decision units
(Departments, programs, activities)

**Step 2:** Create decision packages
(Different service levels with costs)

**Step 3:** Rank all packages
(Prioritize across the organization)

**Step 4:** Allocate resources
(Fund from top of ranking down)

**Key Benefit:** Forces justification of EVERY expense
**Key Drawback:** Time-consuming and politically difficult

sections.title: Activity-Based Budgeting (ABB)
sections.content:
**Reverse of ABC - Budget by Activities:**

**Traditional:** Start with resources, allocate to activities
**ABB:** Start with activities, determine resources needed

**ABB Process:**
1. Forecast sales/production volume
2. Determine activities required
3. Estimate activity quantities
4. Calculate resources needed
5. Budget resource costs

**Example:**
• Forecast: 10,000 orders
• Activity: Process orders (0.5 hrs each)
• Hours needed: 5,000
• Resources: 3 FTEs at $50,000 = $150,000

**Benefit:** Links budget to workload directly

sections.title: Beyond Budgeting Principles
sections.content:
**Philosophy:** Abandon annual budget entirely

**Replace With:**
• **Rolling Forecasts:** Updated quarterly, 12-18 month horizon
• **Relative Targets:** Beat competition, not arbitrary budget
• **KPIs:** Focus on key performance indicators
• **Dynamic Resource Allocation:** Fund as needed

**12 Principles (grouped):**

**Leadership:**
• Purpose over targets
• Values over rules
• Transparency over control

**Process:**
• Goals: relative, adaptive
• Rewards: team-based
• Planning: continuous
• Resources: on demand
• Coordination: market-like

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Method Selection
sections.content:
**Match method to situation:**

**Use ZBB when:**
• Costs have crept up
• Significant restructuring needed
• G&A and discretionary costs
• Leadership willing to challenge status quo

**Use ABB when:**
• Complex, activity-intensive operations
• Need to link resources to workload
• Shared services environment

**Avoid ZBB when:**
• Annual cycle already time-consuming
• Operations are stable and efficient
• Organization lacks analysis capacity

**Beyond Budgeting challenges:**
• Requires cultural change
• Less common, harder to implement

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Traditional budgeting carries forward inefficiencies
ZBB starts from zero - justify every expense
ABB budgets based on activities and cost drivers
Performance-based links budget to outcomes/objectives
Beyond Budgeting replaces annual budget with rolling forecasts + KPIs
Each method fits different situations
ZBB is time-consuming but forces cost justification

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's SG&A costs have increased 40% over 5 years despite flat revenues. Management wants to fundamentally review all support costs. Which budgeting approach is most appropriate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: ZBB is ideal when costs have crept up and need fundamental review. It forces every expense to be justified from zero, eliminating accumulated inefficiencies in G&A costs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: When costs have bloated over time, which method requires justifying everything from scratch?

title: Cash Flow Forecasting
description: Project future cash flows using direct and indirect methods

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Profit doesn't pay bills - cash does! CMAs forecast cash flows to ensure the company can meet obligations, plan financing, and identify investment opportunities. Cash flow forecasting shares methods with the Statement of Cash Flows.

sections.title: Cash Flow Categories
sections.content:
**Operating Cash Flows:**
• Day-to-day business activities
• Cash from sales, payments to suppliers
• Most predictable component

**Investing Cash Flows:**
• Capital expenditures
• Asset acquisitions/disposals
• More discretionary

**Financing Cash Flows:**
• Debt borrowing/repayment
• Equity issuances/buybacks
• Dividends

**Forecasting Focus:**
Operating → Highly detailed
Investing → Known plans
Financing → Balancing item

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Indirect Method Adjustments
sections.content:
**Start with Net Income, then adjust:**

**Add back non-cash expenses:**
+ Depreciation
+ Amortization
+ Loss on sale of assets

**Subtract non-cash income:**
- Gain on sale of assets

**Working capital changes (opposite of B/S change):**
• ↑ Asset = Cash outflow (subtract)
• ↓ Asset = Cash inflow (add)
• ↑ Liability = Cash inflow (add)
• ↓ Liability = Cash outflow (subtract)

**Memory trick:** Increase in A/R = Sold on credit = Cash NOT received = SUBTRACT

sections.title: Direct Method Calculations
sections.content:
**Cash Collected from Customers:**
= Sales + ↓A/R (or - ↑A/R)
= Beginning A/R + Sales - Ending A/R

**Cash Paid to Suppliers:**
= COGS + ↑Inventory + ↓A/P
= COGS + (Inventory change) - (A/P change)

**Cash Paid for Operating Expenses:**
= Operating expenses
+ Prepaid expense increase
- Accrued expense increase
- Depreciation (non-cash)

**Cash Paid for Taxes:**
= Tax expense + ↓Tax payable

**Assemble all to get Operating Cash Flow**

sections.title: Forecasting Best Practices
sections.content:
**Short-Term (< 1 year):**
• Weekly or monthly detail
• Bottom-up from operational plans
• Track individual customer/supplier patterns

**Medium-Term (1-3 years):**
• Quarterly or annual
• Linked to strategic plan
• Key assumptions documented

**Key Inputs:**
• Sales forecast (driver of collections)
• Collection patterns (DSO)
• Payment patterns (DPO)
• Capital expenditure plans
• Debt maturity schedule

**Scenario Analysis:**
• Best case / Base case / Worst case
• Identify cash stress points

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Working Capital Impact
sections.content:
**Growing companies consume cash!**

**As sales grow:**
• A/R increases (not collected yet)
• Inventory increases (more stock needed)
• These USE cash
• Even while reporting profits!

**Example:**
Net Income: $100,000
A/R increase: $50,000
Inventory increase: $30,000
A/P increase: $20,000

**Operating Cash Flow:**
$100,000 - $50,000 - $30,000 + $20,000
= $40,000 (much less than profit!)

**Profitability ≠ Liquidity**
Companies can go bankrupt while profitable!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three categories: Operating, Investing, Financing
Direct method: Shows actual cash receipts and payments
Indirect method: Starts with net income, adjusts for non-cash and WC
Add depreciation (non-cash); Subtract increases in current assets
Growing companies consume cash through working capital
Cash collected = Sales - Increase in A/R
Always distinguish between profitability and cash generation

knowledgeCheck.question: Net income is $200,000. Depreciation is $40,000. Accounts receivable increased by $25,000 and accounts payable increased by $15,000. What is operating cash flow (indirect method)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Start with NI $200,000 + Depreciation $40,000 - A/R increase $25,000 + A/P increase $15,000 = $230,000. Depreciation is added back (non-cash). A/R increase is cash NOT collected. A/P increase is cash NOT paid (good).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Add non-cash expenses, subtract asset increases, add liability increases.

title: Zero-Based Budgeting
description: Build budgets from zero rather than incremental adjustments

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional budgets assume last year's spending was justified! ZBB challenges this by requiring every expense to be justified from zero. CMAs use ZBB to cut waste, reallocate resources, and drive organizational discipline.

sections.title: Traditional vs Zero-Based Budgeting
sections.content:
**Traditional/Incremental Budgeting:**
• Start with last year's budget
• Add % increase
• Quick and easy
• Assumes prior spending was appropriate
• 'Budget creep' over time

**Zero-Based Budgeting:**
• Start from zero each period
• Justify every expense
• Prioritize all activities
• Eliminates waste
• Labor-intensive

**When ZBB Works Best:**
• Discretionary spending
• Administrative functions
• Cost reduction initiatives
• Resource reallocation

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Decision Packages
sections.content:
**Each decision package contains:**

**Activity Description:**
• What does it do?
• Why is it necessary?

**Resource Requirements:**
• People
• Costs
• Equipment

**Alternatives:**
• Different ways to achieve goal
• Different service levels

**Consequences:**
• What if not funded?
• Impact on organization

**Three Service Levels:**
• Minimum (survival)
• Current (status quo)
• Enhanced (improvement)

Management funds from minimum up until budget exhausted

sections.title: ZBB Benefits
sections.content:
**Cost Reduction:**
• Eliminates 'fat' in budgets
• Identifies redundancies
• Stops budget padding

**Strategic Alignment:**
• Links spending to priorities
• Forces strategic discussion
• Resource reallocation

**Engagement:**
• Managers justify activities
• Better understanding of costs
• Ownership of budgets

**Transparency:**
• Clear cost drivers
• Documented decisions
• Audit trail

sections.title: ZBB Challenges
sections.content:
**Time Intensive:**
• Much more work than incremental
• Thousands of decision packages
• Manager time burden

**Resistance:**
• Threatens existing budgets
• Political conflicts
• Gaming the system

**Not Always Practical:**
• Some costs are fixed
• Commitments already made
• Regulatory requirements

**Implementation:**
• May do every 3-5 years
• Focus on discretionary areas
• Use technology to automate

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ZBB vs Activity-Based Budgeting
sections.content:
**Don't confuse these!**

**Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB):**
• Start from zero each period
• Justify every expense
• Prioritization focus
• Decision packages
• Any costing approach

**Activity-Based Budgeting (ABB):**
• Budget based on activities
• Uses ABC cost drivers
• Activity → Resource requirements
• Links to ABC costing

**They can work together:**
• ZBB determines WHAT to fund
• ABB determines HOW MUCH to budget
• Both improve budget accuracy

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ZBB: Start from zero, justify every expense
Traditional: Start with last year + adjustment
Decision packages: Describe activities at different service levels
Process: Identify units, create packages, rank, allocate
Benefits: Cuts waste, aligns spending with strategy
Challenges: Time-intensive, resistance, political issues
Best for: Discretionary spending, periodic deep reviews

knowledgeCheck.question: In zero-based budgeting, decision packages are evaluated and ranked. Funding is typically allocated starting with which service level?
options: Enhanced level first, then work down
Current level to maintain status quo
Minimum level first, then higher levels if funds remain
knowledgeCheck.explanation: ZBB starts by funding minimum service levels for the highest priority activities, then adds enhanced levels if budget remains. This ensures essential functions are covered before discretionary improvements. The ranking process determines which activities get funded beyond minimum.
knowledgeCheck.hint: ZBB builds up from the most essential services.

title: Flexible Budgets
description: Create budgets that adjust for actual activity levels

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Static budgets are misleading when activity levels change! Flexible budgets adjust expected costs for actual volume, providing meaningful variance analysis. CMAs use flex budgets to fairly evaluate performance - managers shouldn't be penalized for volume changes outside their control.

sections.title: Static vs Flexible Budget
sections.content:
**Static Budget:**
• One budgeted activity level
• Doesn't adjust for volume
• Compare actual to original budget
• Volume impacts distort evaluation

**Flexible Budget:**
• Adjusts for actual activity
• 'What SHOULD costs be at actual volume?'
• Separates volume and efficiency
• Fair performance evaluation

**Key Insight:**
Flexible budget = Variable cost per unit × Actual units + Fixed costs

This is what costs SHOULD have been

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Flex Budget Formula
sections.content:
**Flexible Budget = (Variable × Actual Units) + Fixed**

**Example:**
• Variable cost: $5/unit
• Fixed costs: $10,000
• Static budget: 2,000 units
• Actual production: 2,400 units

**Static Budget:**
($5 × 2,000) + $10,000 = $20,000

**Flexible Budget:**
($5 × 2,400) + $10,000 = $22,000

**Actual costs:** $23,500

**Variances:**
• Flex budget: $23,500 - $22,000 = $1,500 U (efficiency)
• Volume: $22,000 - $20,000 = $2,000 U (more production)
• Total: $23,500 - $20,000 = $3,500 U

sections.title: Creating Flexible Budgets
sections.content:
**Step 1: Identify Cost Behavior**
• Variable costs (change with volume)
• Fixed costs (stay constant)
• Mixed costs (separate using high-low, regression)

**Step 2: Determine Variable Rate**
• Per unit, per labor hour, per machine hour
• Depends on cost driver

**Step 3: Determine Fixed Amount**
• Total fixed costs in relevant range

**Step 4: Apply to Actual Activity**
• Calculate what budget SHOULD be
• Compare to actual

**Multiple Drivers:**
• Some costs vary with different drivers
• Build activity-based flex budget

sections.title: Benefits of Flexible Budgets
sections.content:
**Fair Performance Evaluation:**
• Remove volume impacts from manager control
• Focus on controllable items
• Better motivation

**Better Variance Analysis:**
• Separate volume from efficiency
• More actionable insights
• Drill into root causes

**Planning Support:**
• Quick 'what-if' analysis
• Scenario planning
• Range of activity levels

**Communication:**
• Clear cost expectations
• Better resource planning
• Responsive to changes

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Favorable vs Unfavorable
sections.content:
**Know the terminology!**

**Favorable (F):**
• Actual revenue > Budget = F
• Actual cost < Budget = F
• Good for profitability

**Unfavorable (U):**
• Actual revenue < Budget = U
• Actual cost > Budget = U
• Bad for profitability

**Volume Variance:**
• More units than expected = F for revenue
• More units than expected = U for variable costs (expected!)

**Don't confuse direction:**
Higher costs are UNFAVORABLE
Lower costs are FAVORABLE
(regardless of volume change)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Static budget: One activity level; distorts evaluation when volume changes
Flexible budget: Adjusts for actual activity level
Formula: (Variable rate × Actual units) + Fixed costs
Flexible budget variance: Spending/efficiency issues
Volume variance: Impact of activity level change
Requires understanding cost behavior (fixed vs variable)
Favorable: Revenue > budget or Cost < budget

knowledgeCheck.question: Static budget: 10,000 units, $50,000 variable cost, $20,000 fixed. Actual: 12,000 units, $62,000 variable cost, $21,000 fixed. What is the flexible budget variance for variable costs?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Variable rate = $50,000 / 10,000 = $5/unit. Flex budget for 12,000 units = $5 × 12,000 = $60,000. Actual = $62,000. Variance = $62,000 - $60,000 = $2,000 unfavorable. This isolates the efficiency problem from the volume change.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate: Flexible budget = Variable rate × Actual units. Then compare to actual.

title: Rolling Forecasts
description: Implement continuous planning through rolling forecasts

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Static annual budgets are obsolete by month 3! Rolling forecasts provide up-to-date projections that adapt to changing conditions. CMAs lead the shift from annual budget cycles to continuous, agile planning that keeps organizations responsive.

sections.title: Rolling Forecasts Defined
sections.content:
**What is a Rolling Forecast?**
Continuously updated projection that extends a fixed period into the future

**Key Characteristics:**
• Constant planning horizon (e.g., always 12-18 months ahead)
• Updated monthly or quarterly
• Drops oldest period, adds new period
• Focus on key drivers, not detailed line items

**Example:**
• Q1: Forecast covers Q2-Q1 next year (4 quarters)
• Q2: Drop Q2, add Q2 next year (still 4 quarters)
• Always looking 12 months ahead!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Rolling Forecast Design
sections.content:
**Design Decisions:**

**1. Horizon:**
• 12 months (most common)
• 18-24 months (capital intensive)
• 6 months (very dynamic industries)

**2. Update Frequency:**
• Monthly - Most responsive
• Quarterly - Balance effort/value

**3. Detail Level:**
• Driver-based - Revenue drivers, cost drivers
• Not every GL account
• Focus on material items

**4. Granularity:**
• Near-term: Monthly detail
• Far-term: Quarterly or annual

**Rule of Thumb:**
Less detail = easier updates = more likely to happen

sections.title: Implementation Best Practices
sections.content:
**Success Factors:**

**1. Driver-Based:**
• Identify 5-10 key drivers
• Link assumptions to outputs
• Easy to update assumptions

**2. System Support:**
• Planning software
• Not just spreadsheets
• Integration with ERP

**3. Process Discipline:**
• Regular cadence
• Clear ownership
• Time-boxed meetings

**4. Management Engagement:**
• Use forecasts in decisions
• Not just for finance
• Action-oriented discussions

**5. Continuous Improvement:**
• Track forecast accuracy
• Improve driver models
• Learn from variances

sections.title: Beyond Budgeting Movement
sections.content:
**Beyond Budgeting Principles:**
• Adaptive processes, not fixed plans
• Relative targets, not fixed targets
• Decentralized decision-making
• Continuous planning

**Why Challenge Traditional Budgets?**
• Gaming behavior to hit targets
• Spend-it-or-lose-it mentality
• Outdated by mid-year
• Excessive time investment

**Hybrid Approach:**
Many companies keep some budget elements (accountability) while adding rolling forecasts (planning visibility)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Forecasts vs Budgets
sections.content:
**Know the distinction!**

**Budgets:**
• Commitment/target
• Used for accountability
• Often linked to compensation
• 'What we're committing to achieve'

**Forecasts:**
• Best estimate
• Used for planning
• Should be unbiased
• 'What we think will happen'

**Rolling Forecasts:**
• Are FORECASTS, not budgets
• Don't link to compensation
• Want honest predictions
• Avoid sandbagging incentives

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Rolling forecast: Always looks fixed period ahead (12-18 months)
Updates monthly/quarterly; drops old, adds new period
Driver-based: Focus on key assumptions, not line items
Purpose: Planning visibility, not accountability
Beyond budgeting: Movement toward adaptive, continuous planning
Keep forecasts unbiased - don't link to compensation
Hybrid common: Budget for accountability + rolling forecast for planning

knowledgeCheck.question: A company maintains a 12-month rolling forecast updated quarterly. At the end of Q2, what periods should the updated forecast cover?
options: Q3 current year through Q2 next year
Q1 current year through Q4 current year
Q3 current year through Q4 next year
Q2 current year through Q1 next year
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A 12-month rolling forecast always looks 12 months ahead. At end of Q2, it should cover Q3-Q4 current year plus Q1-Q2 next year (four quarters = 12 months). Q2 just completed drops off, and Q2 next year is added.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Rolling forecast always looks the same number of months ahead - 12 months in this case.

title: Operating Budget Components
description: Understand the interrelated operating budgets in the master budget

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: The master budget is the financial plan for the organization! CMAs orchestrate the budget process, ensuring all pieces fit together. Understanding how budgets interrelate is essential for creating cohesive plans and identifying problems.

sections.title: Master Budget Overview
sections.content:
**Master Budget:**
Comprehensive plan for all activities

**Two Main Parts:**

**Operating Budget:**
• Revenue and expenses
• Ends with budgeted income statement

**Financial Budget:**
• Cash budget
• Capital expenditure budget
• Budgeted balance sheet
• Budgeted cash flow statement

**Starting Point:**
Sales budget - everything flows from sales!

**Duration:**
Typically annual, broken into quarters/months

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Production Budget
sections.content:
**Production Budget Formula:**

**Required Production =**
Budgeted Sales
+ Desired Ending Inventory
- Beginning Inventory

**Think:** What you need to have, minus what you start with

**Example:**
• Q1 Sales: 10,000 units
• Desired ending inventory: 2,000
• Beginning inventory: 1,500

**Production needed:**
10,000 + 2,000 - 1,500 = **10,500 units**

**Common Policy:**
Ending inventory = 20% of next period's sales

sections.title: Materials and Labor Budgets
sections.content:
**Direct Materials Purchase Budget:**

Materials needed for production
+ Desired ending materials inventory
- Beginning materials inventory
= Materials to purchase

**Direct Labor Budget:**

Production units
× Labor hours per unit
= Total labor hours needed
× Hourly labor rate
= Total labor cost

**Key Inputs:**
• Standard quantities from engineering
• Standard rates from HR
• Any planned rate increases

sections.title: Overhead and S&A Budgets
sections.content:
**Manufacturing Overhead Budget:**
• Variable OH: Based on activity level
• Fixed OH: Stays constant
• Cash vs non-cash (depreciation)

**Key for Cash Budget:**
Total OH - Depreciation = Cash for OH

**Selling & Administrative Budget:**
• Variable S&A: Commissions, shipping
• Fixed S&A: Salaries, rent, marketing

**Types of Expenses:**
• Marketing/advertising
• Sales salaries and commissions
• Administrative salaries
• Office expenses

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Budget Timing
sections.content:
**Watch the timing differences!**

**Production ≠ Sales:**
• Produce ahead for inventory
• Seasonal preparation

**Purchases ≠ Production:**
• Buy materials ahead
• Lead time for suppliers

**Purchases ≠ Cash Paid:**
• Credit terms (pay next month)
• A/P tracking

**Sales ≠ Cash Received:**
• Credit sales
• Collection patterns

**Key Budget Question:**
'WHEN does cash actually move?'

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Master budget: Operating + Financial budgets
Sales budget: Starting point - everything flows from sales
Production = Sales + Ending inv - Beginning inv
Materials: Similar formula for purchases
Labor: Production units × hours/unit × rate
Overhead: Variable (activity-based) + Fixed
Cash budget: Timing differs from accrual budgets

knowledgeCheck.question: Budgeted sales are 50,000 units. Beginning inventory is 8,000 units. Desired ending inventory is 10,000 units. How many units should be produced?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Production = Sales + Desired Ending Inventory - Beginning Inventory = 50,000 + 10,000 - 8,000 = 52,000 units. The company needs to produce 2,000 more units than it will sell to build inventory.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Production = Sales + Ending - Beginning inventory.

title: Capital Budgeting Fundamentals
description: Understand capital investment decision framework

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capital investments determine a company's future! CMAs analyze and recommend major expenditures. Understanding capital budgeting techniques is essential for supporting strategic investment decisions. This topic bridges planning (CMA1) and investment decisions (CMA2).

sections.title: Capital Budgeting Overview
sections.content:
**What is Capital Budgeting?**
• Multi-year investment decisions
• Large expenditures
• Long-term commitments
• Strategic impact

**Types of Projects:**
• **Expansion:** New capacity, markets
• **Replacement:** Upgrade existing assets
• **Compliance:** Meet regulations
• **R&D:** Innovation and development
• **Other:** IT systems, acquisitions

**Decision Process:**
1. Identify projects
2. Estimate cash flows
3. Evaluate using techniques
4. Select projects
5. Monitor and audit

sections.title: Capital Budgeting Techniques
rows: PV of inflows - PV of outflows
PV of inflows / PV of outflows

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Non-Discounted Methods
sections.content:
**Payback Period:**

**Simple Payback = Investment / Annual Cash Flow**

**Example:**
• Investment: $100,000
• Annual cash flow: $25,000
• Payback = $100,000 / $25,000 = 4 years

**Strengths:**
• Simple, easy to understand
• Measures liquidity risk

**Weaknesses:**
• Ignores time value of money
• Ignores cash flows after payback
• No link to wealth creation

**Accounting Rate of Return:**
ARR = Average net income / Average investment
Average investment = (Initial + Salvage) / 2
• Uses accounting income (not cash)
• Simple but misleading

sections.title: Cash Flow Estimation
sections.content:
**Relevant Cash Flows:**
• **Initial:** Purchase, installation, WC increase
• **Operating:** Revenue - Costs (after-tax)
• **Terminal:** Salvage, WC recovery, cleanup

**Key Principles:**
• Use INCREMENTAL cash flows
• After-tax amounts
• Ignore sunk costs
• Include opportunity costs
• Consider cannibalization

**Depreciation:**
• Not a cash flow itself
• But provides tax shield
• Tax shield = Depreciation × Tax rate

sections.title: Operating Cash Flow
sections.content:
**Three Equivalent Methods:**

**Method 1:**
OCF = (Revenue - Cash costs) × (1-T) + Depreciation × T

**Method 2:**
OCF = Net income + Depreciation

**Method 3:**
OCF = (Revenue - Cash costs - Depreciation) × (1-T) + Depreciation

**Example:**
• Revenue increase: $80,000
• Cash costs increase: $50,000
• Depreciation: $10,000
• Tax rate: 30%

**Method 1:**
OCF = ($80,000 - $50,000)(0.70) + $10,000(0.30)
     = $21,000 + $3,000 = $24,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Sunk Costs and Opportunity Costs
sections.content:
**Sunk Costs - IGNORE!**
• Already spent, can't recover
• Not relevant to decision
• Example: Prior R&D costs

**Opportunity Costs - INCLUDE!**
• Foregone alternatives
• Relevant to decision
• Example: Rent on unused space

**Working Capital:**
• Initial outflow at start
• Recovery at end of project
• Often overlooked!

**Cannibalization:**
• New product taking sales from existing
• Include as cost
• Use incremental revenue only

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital budgeting: Long-term investment decisions
Non-discounted: Payback (liquidity), ARR (simple)
Discounted: NPV, IRR, PI (preferred methods)
Use incremental, after-tax cash flows
Ignore sunk costs, include opportunity costs
Depreciation provides tax shield = Dep × T
Working capital: Invest at start, recover at end

knowledgeCheck.question: A project costs $200,000 and generates $80,000 annual operating cash flow. What is the payback period?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Payback period = Initial investment / Annual cash flow = $200,000 / $80,000 = 2.5 years. This simple method measures how quickly the investment is recovered, but ignores time value of money.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Divide initial investment by annual cash flow.

title: Zero-Based and Activity-Based Budgeting
description: Apply alternative budgeting approaches

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional budgeting perpetuates inefficiency! CMAs must know alternative approaches that challenge the status quo and allocate resources more effectively. Zero-based and activity-based budgeting are powerful tools for cost optimization.

sections.title: Traditional vs Zero-Based
sections.content:
**Traditional (Incremental) Budgeting:**
• Start with last year's budget
• Adjust for expected changes
• 'Last year + X%'
• Fast but perpetuates waste

**Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB):**
• Start from zero each period
• Justify ALL expenditures
• No assumption of continuation
• Thorough but time-consuming

**Key Difference:**
Incremental asks: 'How much more?'
ZBB asks: 'Why spend anything?'

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Zero-Based Budgeting
sections.content:
**ZBB Process:**

**1. Define Decision Units:**
• Identify budget centers
• Usually departments or functions

**2. Create Decision Packages:**
• Describe activity
• State purpose and costs
• Define alternatives
• Identify different funding levels:
  - Minimum (bare essential)
  - Current (maintain)
  - Incremental (improvements)

**3. Evaluate and Rank:**
• Prioritize all packages
• Rank by benefit/importance

**4. Allocate Resources:**
• Fund from top down
• Until budget exhausted
• Low-ranked items cut

sections.title: Activity-Based Budgeting (ABB)
sections.content:
**Based on ABC Costing:**
• Identify activities
• Determine cost drivers
• Budget based on expected activity

**Process:**
1. Forecast production/sales
2. Determine activities required
3. Estimate activity levels
4. Apply cost per activity
5. Calculate budget

**Example:**
• Activity: Machine setups
• Driver: Number of setups
• Cost per setup: $500
• Expected setups: 200
• Budget: 200 × $500 = $100,000

**Benefit:**
Budget reflects actual workload, not history

sections.title: ZBB Advantages and Disadvantages
sections.content:
**Advantages:**
• Eliminates waste and inefficiency
• Forces justification
• Identifies alternatives
• Better resource allocation
• Increases accountability
• Aligns spending with strategy

**Disadvantages:**
• Very time-consuming
• Resource-intensive
• May be confrontational
• Can discourage long-term thinking
• Skilled staff needed
• May not be practical annually

**Practical Application:**
Often done on rotating basis
Different departments each year

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Budget Method Selection
sections.content:
**When to Use Each Method:**

**Incremental:**
• Stable environment
• Limited resources for budgeting
• Mature operations

**Zero-Based:**
• Cost cutting initiatives
• New organization/restructuring
• Support functions (admin, IT)
• When incremental is hiding waste

**Activity-Based:**
• Complex operations
• Significant overhead costs
• Multiple products/services
• When traditional costing distorts

**Common Exam Questions:**
• Identify appropriate method for scenario
• Describe ZBB process
• Compare advantages/disadvantages
• Calculate ABB budget

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Incremental: Last year + adjustments (fast but wasteful)
Zero-based: Start from zero, justify everything
ZBB creates decision packages ranked by priority
Activity-based: Budget based on activities and drivers
ZBB is thorough but time-consuming
ABB aligns budget with actual work performed
Choose method based on situation and objectives

knowledgeCheck.question: Which budgeting approach requires managers to justify ALL expenditures from zero each period?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Zero-based budgeting (ZBB) requires starting from a base of zero and justifying every expenditure for each new period. Unlike incremental budgeting that starts with last year's budget, ZBB does not assume any prior spending should continue.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which method starts from 'zero'?

title: Rolling Forecasts and Continuous Planning
description: Implement dynamic forecasting beyond static budgets

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Static annual budgets become outdated quickly! Rolling forecasts provide continuous visibility into the future, enabling faster responses to change. Modern CMAs champion agile planning techniques that keep organizations ahead.

sections.title: Limitations of Annual Budgets
sections.content:
**Traditional Budget Problems:**
• Outdated within months
• Gaming behavior (spend it or lose it)
• Time-consuming to prepare
• Backward-looking
• Creates fixed targets that may be irrelevant
• Discourages risk-taking

**Business Reality:**
• Markets change rapidly
• Competition is dynamic
• Annual cycles are arbitrary
• Need continuous adaptation

sections.title: Rolling Forecasts Overview
sections.content:
**What is a Rolling Forecast?**
• Continuous projection of future periods
• Updated regularly (monthly/quarterly)
• Maintains constant future horizon
• Always looking 12-18 months ahead

**How It Works:**
1. Start with base forecast
2. Each period, drop completed month
3. Add new month at end
4. Update assumptions
5. Always same horizon (e.g., 18 months)

**Example:**
January: Forecast Feb-Jan (12 months)
February: Drop Jan, add new Feb (12 months)
Always 12 months ahead

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Driver-Based Planning
sections.content:
**Key Concept:**
Focus on key business drivers, not hundreds of line items

**Examples of Drivers:**
• Units sold
• Customers acquired
• Headcount
• Price per unit
• Capacity utilization

**Benefits:**
• Faster to update
• Easier to understand
• Better for scenarios
• Links strategy to operations

**Memory:**
'5%' rule - Focus on the 5% of items that drive 95% of results

Change the driver, model cascades through forecast

sections.title: Implementation Best Practices
sections.content:
**Success Factors:**
• Start simple (5-10 key drivers)
• Technology support (planning tools)
• Clear ownership and process
• Regular cadence (monthly or quarterly)
• Executive sponsorship

**Common Pitfalls:**
• Too much detail (defeats purpose)
• Lack of accountability
• Using forecast for performance evaluation
• Not updating assumptions

**Beyond Budgeting:**
• Movement to eliminate traditional budgets entirely
• Replace with rolling forecasts
• More adaptive management model

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Forecast vs Budget
sections.content:
**Important Distinction:**

**Budget:**
• A TARGET to achieve
• Used for performance evaluation
• Often fixed
• May build in stretch goals

**Forecast:**
• A PREDICTION of outcomes
• Best current estimate
• Updated as conditions change
• Should be realistic, not aspirational

**Why It Matters:**
If forecasts are used for evaluation, managers will game them (sandbag)

**Best Practice:**
Separate target-setting from forecasting
Forecast should be 'most likely' not 'what I want'

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Rolling forecasts maintain constant future horizon
Updated continuously (monthly/quarterly)
Focus on key drivers, not detailed line items
More responsive than static annual budgets
Forecasts predict; budgets target
Driver-based planning simplifies updates
Technology and executive support are critical

knowledgeCheck.question: A company maintains an 18-month rolling forecast updated monthly. After completing February, what happens to the forecast?
options: February drops off, and a new month is added at the end
The entire forecast is rebuilt from scratch
February data replaces the original budget
knowledgeCheck.explanation: In a rolling forecast, after each period ends, it drops off the forecast and a new period is added to maintain the constant horizon. If it's 18 months, after February actuals are recorded, February drops off and August of the following year is added to maintain 18 months visibility.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Rolling forecasts maintain a constant forward-looking horizon.
